fetching.title: Server Fetch
fetching.description: Server Components can fetch data directly — no API routes, no useEffect, no loading spinners. Data is available before the page renders.
fetching.liveDemoDescription: This data was queried from SQLite using Drizzle ORM directly in the Server Component. No API call, no client-side fetching.
fetching.pattern3Description: Avoid waterfalls by fetching data in parallel with Promise.all.
fetching.serverFetchPro1: No loading state — data ready at render
fetching.serverFetchPro2: No client JS for data fetching
fetching.noDefaultCacheDescription: In Next.js 16, fetch() has no default caching. Data is fresh on every request. Use 'use cache' directive to opt into caching.
fetching.autoDedupDescription: Identical fetch() calls in the same render are automatically deduplicated. Multiple components fetching the same URL only make one request.

clientFetch.title: Client Fetch (TanStack Query)
clientFetch.description: For client-side data fetching with caching, refetching, loading states, and error handling. Perfect for interactive data that changes based on user actions.
clientFetch.liveDemo: Live Demo — Fetching from Hono API
clientFetch.liveDemoDescription: This component uses useQuery to fetch posts from /api/posts. Try the refetch button!
clientFetch.setupDescription: TanStack Query needs a QueryClientProvider wrapping your app. This project sets it up in app/providers.tsx.
clientFetch.autoCachingDescription: TanStack Query caches responses by queryKey. Same key = same cached data across components. Configure staleTime to control freshness.
clientFetch.backgroundRefetchDescription: Stale data is shown immediately while fresh data fetches in the background. No loading spinner for cached data — instant UI.
clientFetch.whenToUseDescription: Use client fetch for: real-time data, user-triggered refreshes, infinite scroll, search-as-you-type, or any data that changes based on client state.
clientFetch.serverFetchFirstDescription: Default to Server Components for initial data. Add TanStack Query only when you need client-side interactivity (refetch, polling, optimistic updates).

caching.title: Caching
caching.description: Next.js 16 has NO default caching. You must explicitly opt in using the 'use cache' directive. This gives you full control over what's cached and for how long.
caching.v14v15Description: fetch() was cached by default. You had to opt OUT of caching with cache: 'no-store'. This caused confusion — stale data was a common gotcha.
caching.v16Description: Nothing is cached by default. You opt IN with 'use cache'. Explicit, predictable, no surprises.
caching.useCacheDirectiveDescription: Add 'use cache' to a function or component to cache its result. Works at the function level (like 'use server').
caching.cacheLifeDescription: Set how long the cache should live. Use built-in profiles or custom durations.
caching.cacheTagDescription: Tag cached data so you can invalidate it precisely when data changes.
caching.noDefaultCacheDescription: fetch() and database queries are NOT cached unless you add 'use cache'. This is the opposite of Next.js 14/15.
caching.functionLevelCachingDescription: 'use cache' works at the function level, not the route level. You can cache specific data fetches while keeping others fresh.
caching.argumentsCacheKeyDescription: Function arguments become part of the cache key. getProduct("1") and getProduct("2") have separate cache entries.
caching.requiresConfigDescription: Enable with cacheComponents: true in next.config.ts. This is the new default in Next.js 16 but may need explicit opt-in.

revalidation.title: Revalidation
revalidation.description: Revalidation updates cached data without rebuilding the entire page. Next.js 16 offers time-based (cacheLife) and on-demand (revalidateTag, revalidatePath) strategies.
revalidation.timeBasedDescription: Cache expires after a set duration. After expiry, the next request gets fresh data while stale data is served in the meantime (stale-while-revalidate).
revalidation.onDemandDescription: Manually invalidate cache when data changes — typically inside a Server Action after a mutation (create, update, delete).
revalidation.onDemandSectionDescription: Tag your cached data, then invalidate by tag when data changes.
revalidation.revalidatePathDescription: Invalidate all cached data for a specific URL path. Less precise than tags but simpler.
revalidation.tagOverPathDescription: Prefer revalidateTag over revalidatePath. Tags are precise — you invalidate exactly the data that changed, not an entire page.
revalidation.updateTagForFormsDescription: Use updateTag in Server Actions so the user sees their change immediately after submitting a form. revalidateTag would show stale data first.
revalidation.noCacheNoRevalidationDescription: Revalidation only works with cached data. If you don't use 'use cache', data is always fresh — no revalidation needed.
revalidation.serverActionsOnlyDescription: updateTag only works in Server Actions. revalidateTag works in both Server Actions and Route Handlers.

serverActions.title: Server Actions
serverActions.description: Server Actions are async functions that run on the server. Call them from forms or event handlers to mutate data — no API routes needed.
serverActions.liveDemo: Live Demo — CRUD with Server Actions
serverActions.liveDemoDescription: Create and delete posts using Server Actions. The form submits directly to the server, validates with Zod, and revalidates the page.
serverActions.progressiveEnhancementDescription: Forms with Server Actions work even with JavaScript disabled. The form submits as a regular POST request.
serverActions.alwaysValidateDescription: Server Actions receive raw FormData. Always validate with Zod or similar. Never trust client input — even from your own forms.
serverActions.revalidateAfterMutationDescription: Call revalidatePath or revalidateTag after mutating data to refresh the page with updated content.
serverActions.useActionStateDescription: Replaces the old useFormState. Returns [state, action, isPending] — built-in pending state, no need for useTransition.

forms.title: Form Validation (react-hook-form + zod)
forms.description: Use react-hook-form to manage form state with Zod schema for type-safe validation.
forms.liveDemoDescription: A fully validated contact form using react-hook-form with zodResolver. Try submitting with invalid data to see inline errors.
forms.noSubmission: Fill out the form and submit to see the result.
forms.typeSafetyDescription: Zod schema infers TypeScript types automatically. Your form data is fully typed from schema to submit handler — no manual type definitions needed.
forms.declarativeValidationDescription: Define validation rules once in the Zod schema. react-hook-form applies them automatically — no imperative validation logic in your components.
forms.minimalRerendersDescription: react-hook-form uses uncontrolled inputs by default. Only the fields with errors re-render, keeping your form performant even with many fields.
forms.composableSchemasDescription: Zod schemas can be composed, extended, and reused. Share validation logic between client forms and server actions for consistent validation.

streaming.title: Streaming
streaming.description: Streaming lets you progressively render UI. Wrap slow components in Suspense to show a fallback instantly while the content loads.
streaming.liveDemo: Live Demo — Watch Components Stream In
streaming.liveDemoDescription: Posts load after ~2s, Stats after ~4s. The rest of the page is instant. Refresh to see it again!
streaming.loadingTsxDescription: Create a loading.tsx file and Next.js automatically wraps the page in a Suspense boundary.
streaming.granularBoundariesDescription: Each Suspense boundary streams independently. Wrap individual slow components, not the entire page — this maximizes perceived performance.
streaming.nestedSuspenseDescription: Suspense boundaries can be nested. Outer shows first, then inner boundaries resolve progressively. Great for complex layouts.
streaming.noClientJsDescription: Streaming works with Server Components only. The server sends HTML chunks as they become ready — no JavaScript framework needed on the client.
streaming.loadingPageSuspenseDescription: loading.tsx automatically wraps the page in Suspense. It shows during navigation before the page component renders.
